sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=28).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 123, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 122 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 4.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After clicking on the B center agent, the active agent changed. Looking at the image:
- The agent at the top-left of the left panel now has a B center (was W before)
- The agent in the connector now has a W center (was B before)
So clicking on an agent swaps the active/inactive status AND swaps their centers! This is a key insight.
Current state:
- id=1: N agent at rows 21-23, cols 9-11, W center - top-left of left panel (was active before, now inactive with W center)
Wait, no. Looking at the segmentation:
- id=1: N agent at rows 21-23, cols 9-11, children=[5], W center (id=5)
- id=8: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 27-29, children=[10], B center (id=10)
So the agent at top-left has W center, and the agent in connector has B center. But the image shows:
- Top-left agent: green border (active), B center (dark)
- Connector agent: green border (active?), W center (light)
Wait, both agents have green borders in the image? That's strange. Let me look more carefully.
Actually, looking at the image, both agents have green borders. But only one should be active. Maybe the green border indicates something else, like the agent's color (N = light green).
Let me re-examine. The N color is light green. So the agents are light green with different center colors. The "active" indicator might be something else, like a selection highlight.
Looking at the image:
- Top-left agent: light green border, dark center (B)
- Connector agent: light green border, light center (W)
But the segmentation says:
- id=1: W center at top-left
- id=8: B center at connector
This is confusing. Let me re-read the segmentation.
id=1: N agent at rows 21-23, cols 9-11, children=[5], W center (id=5)
id=8: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 27-29, children=[10], B center (id=10)
So the top-left agent has W center, and the connector agent has B center. But the image shows the opposite?
Wait, maybe I'm misreading the image. Let me look again.
The top-left agent has a dark center (B), and the connector agent has a light center (W). But the segmentation says the top-left agent has W center and the connector agent has B center.
This is a discrepancy. Maybe the segmentation is from before the MOUSE action, and the action changed the centers?
Actually, looking at the result, the board changed (board_changed: true). So the MOUSE action did something.
Let me check what changed. Before the MOUSE:
- Top-left agent: W center
- Connector agent: B center
After the MOUSE:
- Top-left agent: W center (id=1, children=[5], W)
- Connector agent: B center (id=8, children=[10], B)
Wait, the segmentation is the same? But the image shows different centers.
Actually, I think I'm misreading the image. Let me look more carefully.
The top-left agent has a small dark square in the center. The connector agent has a small light square in the center. But the segmentation says the top-left agent has W (white) center and the connector agent has B (black) center.
Hmm, W is white and B is black. In the image, the top-left agent's center looks dark (B), and the connector agent's center looks light (W). But the segmentation says the opposite.
Wait, maybe the image is showing the agents correctly, and I'm misreading the segmentation. Let me re-read.
id=1: N agent at rows 21-23, cols 9-11, children=[5]
id=5: W, pixels=1, rows[22-22], cols[10-10]
So id=5 is the W center inside id=1 (top-left agent). This means the top-left agent has a W (white) center.
id=8: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 27-29, children=[10]
id=10: B, pixels=1, rows[31-31], cols[28-28]
So id=10 is the B center inside id=8 (connector agent). This means the connector agent has a B (black) center.
But the image shows the top-left agent with a dark center and the connector agent with a light center. This is the opposite of what the segmentation says.
Wait, maybe the image is rendered differently. Let me look at the ASCII representation.
Actually, I think the issue is that the image shows the agents with their centers, but the centers are small and hard to distinguish. The segmentation is more reliable.
So the top-left agent has a W (white) center, and the connector agent has a B (black) center.
Now, after clicking on the connector agent, what happened? The board changed, but the segmentation shows the same centers. So maybe the click didn't swap the centers, but did something else.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, both agents now have green borders. Before, only one had a green border. So maybe the click activated both agents? Or maybe the click changed something about the agents' appearance.
Wait, I think the green border is just the N (light green) color of the agents. Both agents are N-colored, so they both have green borders. The "active" indicator might be something else, like a selection highlight that's not visible in the segmentation.
Let me try moving the connector agent (B center) RIGHT into the right panel. If it's the active agent, it should move.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving the connector agent RIGHT into the right panel
# If it's active, it should move
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
